project: Inner Space vs Outer Space: The Data Center Battle Nobody's Talking About

scenes.title: Cold Open — Earth from Orbit
scenes.narration: In December 2025, a startup called Starcloud put a single NVIDIA H100 GPU into orbit. Sixty kilograms. One chip. They trained a small language model on it, and investors responded with a hundred and seventy million dollars and a billion-dollar valuation.
scenes.visual: Slow orbital shot of Earth from space. Dawn light creeps across the Pacific Ocean. Camera drifts downward toward the blue surface.
scenes.image_prompt: Cinematic view of Earth from low orbit at dawn, the sun cresting over the Pacific Ocean casting golden light across deep blue water and white cloud formations, the curve of the Earth visible, stars fading in the black sky above, photorealistic, dramatic lighting, 16:9 widescreen aspect ratio, no text, no UI elements
scenes.text_overlay: $170M raised. 1 GPU in orbit.

scenes.title: The Filing — Text Reveal
scenes.narration: Two months later, they filed with the FCC to build a constellation of eighty-eight thousand satellites. That would be nearly nine times the size of Starlink. Not for internet. For computing.
scenes.visual: Dark screen. Text types itself on screen in clean white monospace font, one line at a time.
scenes.image_prompt: Title card — black background with white monospace text
scenes.text_overlay: 88,000 satellites. / Filed with the FCC. / February 2026.

scenes.title: The Displacement — Workers Leaving
scenes.narration: Meanwhile, on the other side of this story, seventy thousand offshore oil workers in the UK have lost their jobs in the last decade. In the Gulf of Mexico, nearly a hundred thousand more. Entire communities built around the ocean — the rigs, the supply boats, the welding shops — are watching their industry die. And almost nobody is connecting these two facts.
scenes.visual: Aerial shot of an aging North Sea oil platform in grey, choppy water. A crew boat pulls away from the platform, heading toward shore.
scenes.image_prompt: Aerial photograph of a large offshore oil platform in the grey choppy North Sea, a small crew transfer vessel pulling away from the platform heading toward distant shore, the platform standing isolated and industrial against an overcast sky, the mood is one of departure and ending, documentary photography style, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: UK North Sea: 70,000+ jobs lost / Gulf of Mexico: ~95,000 jobs lost
scenes.music: Shift to low melancholy note, single instrument

scenes.title: Title Card
scenes.narration: This is that story.
scenes.visual: Cinematic split — left half: satellite in orbit against black space, right half: offshore platform with wind turbines on horizon. Thin white dividing line.
scenes.image_prompt: Dramatic cinematic split composition, left half shows a small silver satellite with solar panels extended floating in the blackness of space with stars behind it, right half shows an offshore oil platform with modern wind turbines visible on the horizon rising from dark blue ocean water, a thin vertical white line divides the two halves, moody and cinematic lighting, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: INNER SPACE vs OUTER SPACE — The Data Center Battle Nobody's Talking About
scenes.music: Title theme hit, settle into background
scenes.notes: The narration is broken on this one. Also, General Note: Reduce the time between segments; it seems too long.

scenes.title: The VC Narrative
scenes.narration: To be fair, the outer space pitch is compelling. In orbit, solar panels generate roughly five times more energy than on the ground — no clouds, no night cycle in a sun-synchronous orbit, no atmosphere to filter the light. Radiative cooling to the vacuum of space means you never need water for cooling. And you never have to negotiate with a county planning board for a building permit.
scenes.visual: Sleek rendering of a satellite array in orbit, solar panels extended, Earth below. Clean, aspirational — pitch deck aesthetic.
scenes.image_prompt: Sleek futuristic satellite data center in orbit above Earth, large solar panel arrays extending symmetrically from a central module covered in blinking blue LED indicators, Earth visible below as a blue-white backdrop, clean aspirational tech aesthetic like a startup pitch deck rendering, photorealistic, dramatic rim lighting, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Solar: 5x more efficient in orbit

scenes.title: The Players
scenes.narration: The money is real. Starcloud, backed by Benchmark and EQT, has raised two hundred million and is building its second satellite for October launch. Orbital, funded by Andreessen Horowitz just this month, is targeting an April 2027 test flight with NVIDIA GPUs. Thales, the European defense giant, has a concept design for a ten-megawatt orbital data center — but their target date is 2036. And Google and NVIDIA have run exploratory joint tests.
scenes.visual: Clean company logo cards appearing one at a time against dark background with key stats.
scenes.image_prompt: Title card — dark background with company cards
scenes.text_overlay: Starcloud — $200M, $1.1B valuation / Orbital (a16z) — Seed, Apr 2027 / Thales — 2036 target / Google/NVIDIA — Exploratory

scenes.title: The Ambition — Dyson Sphere Tease
scenes.narration:
The long-term vision goes even further. Some proponents invoke the Dyson sphere — the theoretical structure that captures all energy from a star. Space data centers, they argue, are the first step toward that future.

It is a beautiful idea. But there is a problem.
scenes.visual: Massive orbital data center — solar arrays spanning hundreds of meters, glowing blue LEDs on server modules against the blackness of space. Aspirational and grand.
scenes.image_prompt: Majestic concept art of a massive orbital data center complex in space, enormous solar arrays spanning hundreds of meters connected to server modules, soft blue and white lighting glowing against the void of space, Earth small in the background, science fiction meets engineering concept art style, epic scale, aspirational and grand, 16:9 widescreen, no text

scenes.title: The Cost Bomb
scenes.narration: A ten-megawatt data center in outer space — roughly enough to run a mid-size AI training cluster — would cost between one point six and eleven billion dollars in capital alone. Its operational lifespan? Two to five years. There is no maintenance. When a component fails, the entire satellite is a write-off. You cannot send a technician to low Earth orbit.
scenes.visual: Two bar chart columns. Left 'Outer Space' in red, right 'Inner Space' in blue. Numbers animate in.
scenes.image_prompt: Clean modern data visualization graphic on a dark navy background, two vertical bar chart columns side by side, the left column tall and colored red-orange labeled 'Outer Space', the right column much shorter and colored ocean blue labeled 'Inner Space', clean sans-serif typography, minimal design, infographic style, 16:9 widescreen, no other text
scenes.text_overlay: 10MW Outer Space DC: $1.6-11B / Lifespan: 2-5 years / Maintenance: impossible

scenes.title: The Inner Space Alternative
scenes.narration: A ten-megawatt data center in inner space — floating on an offshore wind platform or housed inside a repurposed oil rig — costs between a hundred and sixty and five hundred thirty million dollars. It lasts twenty-five to thirty years. And when something breaks, you dispatch a remotely operated vehicle. An ROV. Thirty thousand dollars a day to rent. They already exist by the thousands.
scenes.visual: Same chart format. Blue 'Inner Space' column fills in with dramatically lower numbers.
scenes.image_prompt: Same clean data visualization style, dark navy background, two bar chart columns, left red-orange column for Outer Space, right ocean blue column for Inner Space now prominent with positive data filled in, infographic style, 16:9 widescreen, no text other than column labels Outer Space and Inner Space
scenes.text_overlay: 10MW Inner Space DC: $160-530M / Lifespan: 25-30 years / Maintenance: ROV, $30K/day

scenes.title: Twenty-Five Year Math
scenes.narration: Over twenty-five years, the total cost of ownership for a ten-megawatt outer space data center exceeds twenty-five billion dollars. Replacements, launches, orbital decay, insurance — if you can even get it. The same capacity in the ocean? Three to four billion, total. Platform, power, cooling, maintenance, everything.
scenes.visual: Timeline 0-25 years. 'Outer Space' line rockets upward. 'Inner Space' line stays flat. Gap widens dramatically.
scenes.image_prompt: Clean cost comparison chart on dark background, horizontal timeline from 0 to 25 years along the bottom, two plotted lines: a red line labeled Outer Space that climbs steeply upward in jagged steps, a blue line labeled Inner Space that rises modestly then flattens, the gap between them widening dramatically over time, clean infographic design, 16:9 widescreen
scenes.text_overlay: 25-year TCO: Outer Space $25B+ vs. Inner Space $3-4B

scenes.title: The Latency Problem
scenes.narration: And then there is latency. A data center in low Earth orbit adds twenty to fifty milliseconds of round-trip delay. For AI inference — the workload that actually makes money, the one that answers your questions and generates your images in real time — that delay is a dealbreaker. An ocean data center a hundred and thirty kilometers offshore? Zero point six five milliseconds. Effectively invisible.
scenes.visual: Signal path diagram. Outer space arc up 600km. Inner space runs 130km along seafloor cable.
scenes.image_prompt: Technical diagram on dark background showing two signal paths from a ground user to a data center, the first path arcs upward 600km into space to a satellite and back down with a red dotted line labeled Outer Space, the second path runs horizontally 130km along the ocean floor through a submarine cable with a blue solid line labeled Inner Space, clean engineering diagram style with distance markers, 16:9 widescreen
scenes.text_overlay: Outer Space: 20-50ms / Inner Space: +0.65ms

scenes.title: The Natick Story
scenes.narration:
In 2018, Microsoft sank eight hundred and sixty-four servers to the bottom of the ocean off Scotland. Project Natick. They sealed them in a nitrogen-filled capsule, dropped it thirty-five meters down, and left it there for two years.

The results were remarkable. The failure rate was eight times lower than an equivalent land-based data center. Eight times. The cold, stable temperature of the deep ocean and the absence of human intervention — no one opening doors, no dust, no humidity fluctuations — made the servers dramatically more reliable.
scenes.visual: Microsoft Project Natick capsule — white cylindrical pod on the seafloor, fiber optic cables trailing upward through murky green water, small fish swimming nearby.
scenes.image_prompt: Photorealistic rendering of Microsoft Project Natick underwater data center, a large white cylindrical capsule with porthole windows glowing blue-green sitting on the sandy ocean floor 35 meters deep, fiber optic cables trailing upward through murky green water toward the surface, small fish swimming nearby, underwater photography lighting, moody and atmospheric, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Project Natick: 864 servers, 35m deep, 2 years / 8x lower failure rate / PUE: 1.07

scenes.title: The Natick Pivot
scenes.narration:
Microsoft cancelled Natick in 2024. Not because it failed — it was one of the most successful infrastructure experiments of the decade. They cancelled it because they needed hyperscale campus capacity for AI, and the pod model did not fit that strategy. The technology was proven. The business case just pointed somewhere else.

But other companies were paying attention.
scenes.visual: Natick capsule lifted from ocean, dripping water, barnacles on hull. Transition to Azure data center campus.
scenes.image_prompt: A large cylindrical underwater data center capsule being lifted from the ocean by a crane on a ship, water cascading off the white hull, barnacles and marine growth visible on the surface, grey overcast sky, industrial maritime scene, photorealistic, documentary style, 16:9 widescreen, no text

scenes.title: The Companies in the Water
scenes.narration:
In Hainan, China, Highlander has deployed the world's first commercial underwater data center. Operational right now — twelve hundred servers in sealed capsules on the seafloor, with plans for a hundred capsules. They have connected a separate facility near Shanghai directly to an offshore wind farm. The first wind-powered underwater data center on Earth.

In the North Sea, Aikido Technologies is deploying a prototype this year — a floating wind platform with a data center built into the ballast tanks underneath. NVIDIA is a partner. Their commercial launch targets the UK in 2028. Projected PUE? One point zero eight.

In California, Nautilus Data Technologies built a seven-megawatt floating data center in Stockton — a barge that uses river water for cooling. Eighty-six percent occupied.

In the Gulf of Mexico, Hyperscale Data ran a data center on a repurposed oil platform from November 2024 through February 2025 — proving the concept with existing infrastructure.
scenes.visual: World map with glowing dots at each location. Lines connecting offshore sites to nearby cities.
scenes.image_prompt: World map in a dark modern flat design style with glowing cyan dots at the following locations: Hainan Island China, Shanghai coast China, North Sea Norway, Stockton California USA, and Gulf of Mexico, thin glowing lines connecting offshore dots to nearby coastal cities, dark navy ocean background with subtle grid, documentary infographic style, 16:9 widescreen, no text labels
scenes.text_overlay: Highlander — Hainan (OPERATIONAL) / Aikido — North Sea (2026) / Nautilus — Stockton CA (7MW) / Hyperscale Data — Gulf of Mexico

scenes.title: Underwater Pods
scenes.narration: Below the surface, NetworkOcean — a Y Combinator startup — is testing a one-megawatt underwater capsule in San Francisco Bay. Subsea Cloud has projects in Washington State, the Gulf of Mexico, and the North Sea. These are not concepts. They are in the water.
scenes.visual: Sleek underwater data center capsules on ocean floor — cylindrical pods with blue LEDs, cables connecting them, fish swimming past.
scenes.image_prompt: Futuristic underwater data center installation on the ocean floor, multiple sleek cylindrical pods with blue LED indicator lights arranged in a row on a sandy seabed, fiber optic cables connecting them, small schools of fish swimming between the pods, bioluminescent blue-green underwater lighting, photorealistic, atmospheric and moody, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: NetworkOcean — YC W24, SF Bay / Subsea Cloud — WA, GoM, North Sea

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: The Cable Map
scenes.narration: Here is something most people do not realize. Ninety-nine percent of all international internet traffic already travels through the ocean. One point five million kilometers of submarine fiber-optic cable sit on the seafloor right now. The backbone of the internet is not in the cloud. It is in inner space. An ocean data center does not need a new network. It plugs into the one that already exists.
scenes.visual: Animated submarine cable map — thousands of glowing lines crisscrossing every ocean like a neural network.
scenes.image_prompt: Visualization of the global submarine fiber optic cable network, dark world map with thousands of glowing electric blue and cyan cable lines crisscrossing every ocean connecting all continents, the cables glow like a neural network, dense nodes visible at major coastal cities like New York London Tokyo Singapore, dark navy background, data visualization style, dramatic and impressive scale, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: 1.5 million km of submarine cable / 99% of international data traffic

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: The Platform Problem
scenes.narration:
There are twelve thousand offshore oil and gas platforms in the world. Many are approaching end of life. And here is the absurdity: governments are about to spend staggering amounts of money to destroy them. The UK alone has budgeted forty-four billion pounds — fifty-six billion dollars — to decommission its North Sea platforms. The Gulf of Mexico faces forty to seventy billion in demolition costs.

Every one of those platforms already has gas turbines for power, cranes for lifting equipment, helipads for access, and seawater intakes for cooling. Everything a data center needs. We are paying to demolish ready-made inner space data center sites while pouring venture capital into outer space ones.
scenes.visual: Aging offshore oil platform — rusting steel, gas flare burning, grey sky. Then graphic: demolition cost vs repurposing.
scenes.image_prompt: Aging offshore oil and gas platform in the North Sea, rusting steel structure rising from grey choppy ocean water, a gas flare burning orange at the top, overcast stormy sky, industrial decay meets ocean power, documentary photography style, moody and dramatic, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: UK decommissioning: $56B / Gulf of Mexico: $40-70B / 12,000 platforms globally

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: The Workers Who Built These Platforms
scenes.narration:
And those platforms were not built by software engineers. They were built by marine electricians, ROV operators, subsea cable crews, crane operators, and platform managers. The same people who are now being laid off at a rate of four hundred every two weeks in the UK alone. Aberdeen — the oil capital of Europe — has lost eighteen thousand jobs since 2014. It is one of only two cities in the entire UK with fewer jobs than it had in 2010.

In Louisiana, twenty-five thousand offshore workers have lost their jobs since 2014. Oil went from a third of the state economy to fourteen percent. In Norway, Equinor expects ten thousand employees to retire by 2030 — the largest restructuring in the company's twenty-year history. Forty-eight percent of US energy workers are forty-five or older, aging out of an industry with no replacement pipeline.
scenes.visual: Close-up portraits of offshore workers — hard hats, high-vis, weathered faces. ROV control room. Crane operator. Documentary feel — competence and dignity.
scenes.image_prompt: Documentary-style portrait composition of offshore oil workers on a platform deck, three workers in orange high-visibility coveralls and white hard hats, weathered faces, one operating an ROV control console with multiple screens, another standing near heavy crane rigging, the North Sea visible in the background through rain-spattered windows, warm interior lighting contrasting with grey exterior, the mood is competence and quiet dignity, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Aberdeen: 18,000 jobs lost since 2014 / Louisiana: 25,000+ lost, oil 33%→14% / 400 UK jobs lost every 2 weeks

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: The Skills That Already Exist
scenes.narration:
But here is what makes this story so frustrating. These workers already have the skills. An ROV operator who inspected subsea pipelines for thirty years can inspect subsea data center pods with the exact same equipment. A subsea engineer who designed pressure vessels for oil? The same pressure vessels house underwater servers. Marine electricians, subsea cable crews, crane operators — these are not adjacent skills. They are identical jobs with a different mission.

OEUK — the UK's offshore energy trade body — found that ninety percent of oil and gas skills are directly transferable. Not fifty. Not seventy. Ninety.

One offshore worker put it simply: 'As long as it paid the bills, it wouldn't matter to me. If I can come home to my family, that's what matters.' A union engineer said: 'Rigging is rigging. It doesn't matter if you're on an oil rig, in a fabrication yard, or on a wind turbine.'
scenes.visual: Side-by-side comparison: Oil & Gas Role → Ocean DC Role. Each pair connected by lines that light up.
scenes.image_prompt: Clean infographic on dark navy background showing a side-by-side skills comparison, left column labeled 'Oil and Gas' with icons for ROV operator, subsea engineer, marine electrician, cable crew, crane operator, platform manager, right column labeled 'Ocean Data Center' with the same icons mirrored, horizontal connecting lines between each pair glowing cyan, the visual message is that these are the same skills, clean modern flat design, 16:9 widescreen, no text labels
scenes.text_overlay: 90% directly transferable — OEUK / ROV → ROV / Subsea eng → Subsea DC eng / Cable crew → Cable crew (IDENTICAL)
scenes.notes: At about 9:40, the video goes black until 10:07

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: The Flare Gas Play
scenes.narration: Right now, the global oil industry flares a hundred and fifty-one billion cubic meters of natural gas every year. Just burns it. Wasted. That is enough energy to power every data center in the United States. Crusoe Energy proved the model on land — data centers next to wellheads, capturing flared gas for electricity. Sixty-three percent CO2 reduction versus just letting it burn. Two hundred fifty megawatts deployed. Now worth billions.
scenes.visual: Nighttime satellite image of gas flares — bright orange dots across dark landscape. Transition to Crusoe Energy modular units.
scenes.image_prompt: Satellite view at night showing dozens of bright orange gas flares burning across an oil field landscape or offshore region, the flares are pinpoints of intense light scattered across dark terrain, reminiscent of real NASA nighttime satellite imagery of gas flaring in the Permian Basin or Gulf of Mexico, dark background, dramatic contrast, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: 151 BCM flared annually = enough to power every US DC / Crusoe: 63% CO2 reduction, 250+ MW
scenes.notes: The image's naration started at 10:53 the prevouse image was still on screen, we have a sync issue

scenes.chapter_title: The Infrastructure Nobody Wants to Waste
scenes.title: Offshore Wind Scale
scenes.narration: And offshore wind is no longer experimental. Eighty-four point five gigawatts of capacity is installed worldwide today. That is nearly enough to power every data center on Earth. The energy is already being generated. It just needs somewhere to go.
scenes.visual: Sweeping aerial of massive offshore wind farm — dozens of turbines to the horizon, dramatic sky.
scenes.image_prompt: Sweeping aerial photograph of a massive offshore wind farm, dozens of white wind turbines stretching in rows to the horizon on a deep blue ocean, dramatic golden sunset light illuminating the turbine blades, ocean spray and scattered clouds, the scale is immense and impressive, drone photography style, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: 84.5 GW offshore wind installed globally

scenes.title: The Water Crisis
scenes.narration:
Land-based data centers have a water problem that is getting worse every year. In 2023, US data centers consumed an estimated sixty-six billion liters of water directly — and eight hundred billion liters indirectly through electricity generation. Google alone used eight point one billion gallons. Two-thirds of new data center construction is happening in water-stressed regions.

An ocean data center uses zero freshwater. The ocean is the heat sink. Cold seawater flows through heat exchangers and returns a few degrees warmer. No evaporative cooling towers. No municipal water draw. No competing with farms and families for a resource that is running out.
scenes.visual: Split screen. Left: massive DC in desert with cooling towers steaming. Right: low-water reservoir.
scenes.image_prompt: Split composition showing environmental contrast, left half shows a massive modern data center facility in an arid desert landscape with large cooling towers emitting steam rising into a hazy sky near Phoenix Arizona, right half shows a calm blue ocean surface stretching to the horizon with a small offshore platform visible in the distance, the split emphasizes the contrast between water-intensive land computing and water-free ocean computing, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: US DCs: 66B liters direct (2023) / 2/3 in water-stressed areas / Ocean DCs: zero freshwater

scenes.title: The Outer Space Pollution Problem
scenes.narration:
Now consider what eighty-eight thousand satellites would do to the sky. Each Falcon 9 launch deposits roughly four hundred and twenty-five tonnes of CO2 into the atmosphere. But the real damage is the black carbon — soot particles injected directly into the stratosphere, where they are five hundred times more effective at trapping heat than the same particles at ground level.

A 2025 study from ETH Zurich found that projected launch rates will measurably slow the recovery of the ozone layer. Every satellite that reaches end of life burns up on reentry, scattering rare earth metals and aluminum oxide as aerosols into the upper atmosphere. No recycling. No recovery. One hundred percent e-waste, vaporized into the air we breathe.
scenes.visual: Rocket launch ascending, massive exhaust plume. Transition to orbital debris visualization.
scenes.image_prompt: Dramatic photograph of a rocket launch ascending through the atmosphere leaving a massive billowing exhaust plume that spreads wide against a dark blue sky, the exhaust trail is prominent and alarming in scale, emphasizing the atmospheric pollution impact, shot from below looking up, photorealistic documentary style, moody and ominous, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Each Falcon 9: ~425t CO2 / Black carbon: 500x more warming / 100% e-waste on reentry

scenes.title: Kessler Syndrome
scenes.narration: There are already fifty-four thousand tracked objects in orbit. The predicted interval between significant collision events — the CRASH clock — sits at two point eight days. Adding eighty-eight thousand more objects to that environment is not a calculated risk. It is a bet against physics.
scenes.visual: Orbital debris around Earth — thousands of objects forming a dense shell, some colliding and creating expanding clouds.
scenes.image_prompt: Visualization of orbital debris around Earth, thousands of small bright objects forming a dense shell around the planet, viewed from a distance showing Earth partially obscured by the debris field, some objects shown colliding and creating expanding debris clouds, dark space background, scientific visualization style, alarming density, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: 54,000 tracked objects / CRASH clock: 2.8 days / +88,000 proposed

scenes.title: Inner Space Environmental Wins
scenes.narration: Meanwhile, in inner space, something unexpected happens when you put structures in the water. Oil platform bases in the Gulf of Mexico have become some of the most productive artificial reefs on the planet — twelve to fourteen thousand fish per structure. The Rigs-to-Reefs program has been converting decommissioned platforms into permanent marine habitats for decades. A data center on a platform does not just preserve the reef. It funds its protection.
scenes.visual: Beautiful underwater shot — thriving marine ecosystem around artificial reef (oil platform base). Colorful coral, thousands of fish, sunlight filtering down.
scenes.image_prompt: Beautiful underwater photograph of a thriving marine ecosystem around the steel legs of an offshore oil platform converted to an artificial reef, the massive rusted steel structures are covered in colorful coral and sea anemones, large schools of tropical fish swimming between the beams, shafts of sunlight filtering down through blue-green water, National Geographic underwater photography style, vibrant and life-affirming, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: 12,000-14,000 fish per platform / Rigs-to-Reefs: decades of results

scenes.title: The Dyson Sphere Line
scenes.narration: Look — I respect the ambition. The dream of computing in outer space is genuinely inspiring. Someday, maybe. But a Dyson sphere is not a roadmap. It is a thought experiment. Meanwhile, offshore wind is already spinning. Submarine cables are already carrying your data. Oil platforms are already standing in the water, waiting for their next job.
scenes.visual: Split screen fading — left: fantastical Dyson sphere, right: real offshore wind turbine. Rendering fades to translucent as reality becomes dominant.
scenes.image_prompt: Split composition fading from fantasy to reality, left side shows a fantastical science fiction rendering of a partial Dyson sphere structure around a glowing star in deep space with massive solar collection panels, right side shows a real-looking modern offshore wind turbine standing in the ocean against a blue sky with clouds, the left side is slightly translucent and fading as if dissolving into the more solid real-world right side, 16:9 widescreen, no text

scenes.title: The Funding Gap
scenes.narration: Two hundred million dollars bought one GPU in orbit. That same capital could fund multiple megawatt-scale ocean data centers that are operational today. Starcloud has raised money to build a second satellite. Aikido has an NVIDIA partnership and a prototype going into the water this year. Highlander is already running commercial operations. Inner space is not a concept. It is a business.
scenes.visual: Infographic. Left: 'OUTER SPACE' — $200M for 1 GPU. Right: 'INNER SPACE' — stack of operational facilities.
scenes.image_prompt: Clean infographic comparison on dark background, left side labeled OUTER SPACE shows a single small satellite icon with a large dollar sign, right side labeled INNER SPACE shows a stack of icons representing multiple operational facilities including a floating platform, underwater capsule, and wind turbine, the inner space side is visually denser and more substantial, minimal flat design style, 16:9 widescreen, no other text
scenes.text_overlay: $200M = 1 GPU in orbit / vs. / $200M = multiple MW-scale inner space DCs

scenes.title: The Contrast — Two Workforces
scenes.narration:
Outer space data centers are built in Los Angeles, Redmond, Cape Canaveral, Silicon Valley. They need aerospace engineers, orbital mechanics specialists, satellite systems designers. Skills that do not exist in oil communities. They employ hundreds of highly specialized engineers in cities that already have plenty of jobs.

Inner space data centers are built by marine technicians, ROV operators, subsea engineers — people who already live in Aberdeen, Houma, Stavanger, and a hundred other coastal towns. They do not need to move to California. They do not need to retrain for a career they have never heard of. They stay where they are and keep doing what they know, with a new mission.
scenes.visual: Split screen — left: gleaming aerospace campus (Silicon Valley), right: rain-lashed North Sea port with cranes and supply vessels. Two worlds.
scenes.image_prompt: Dramatic split composition showing two different workplaces, left half shows a clean modern aerospace campus with glass buildings and manicured grounds in sunny Silicon Valley California, an engineer in a white clean room suit visible through a window, right half shows a rain-lashed North Sea industrial port with cranes and orange supply vessels, workers in high-visibility gear walking on a wet dock, the contrast between the two worlds is stark but neither is diminished, documentary photography style, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Outer Space: Aerospace — LA, Redmond, Cape Canaveral / Inner Space: Marine technicians — Aberdeen, Houma, Stavanger

scenes.title: The Multiplier — Community Ripple
scenes.narration:
And this is not just about the workers on the platform. Each offshore energy job supports roughly one point eight additional onshore jobs — the supply boat crews, the port workers, the welding shops, the diners. In deepwater, that multiplier rises to four. Aberdeen, the oil capital of Europe, is searching for a new identity. Houma, Louisiana — the offshore capital of the US Gulf — has the same supply boats that could service data center pods tomorrow.

There is a pay gap. Experienced oil workers earn a hundred to two hundred thousand dollars. Offshore wind pays around seventy-two thousand at the ninetieth percentile. But ocean data centers are higher-margin operations than wind farms. They could plausibly close that gap. And a job that exists beats a perfect job that does not.
scenes.visual: Ripple effect infographic — central offshore worker icon with expanding concentric rings of supporting jobs.
scenes.image_prompt: Animated-style infographic on dark background showing a community ripple effect, a central icon of an offshore worker in hard hat and high-vis, surrounded by expanding concentric rings, each ring contains smaller icons representing supporting jobs: supply boat, port crane, welding torch, fuel pump, restaurant, school, the rings glow warmer as they expand outward suggesting community vitality, clean flat design, 16:9 widescreen, no text labels
scenes.text_overlay: Each offshore job supports 1.8 onshore jobs (OEUK) / Deepwater: 4x (BOEM) / Oil: $100-200K | Wind: ~$72K | Ocean DCs: higher margin

scenes.title: The Crossover
scenes.narration:
But here is the part of the story nobody saw coming. In 2025, according to Rystad Energy, global capital expenditure on data centers reached seven hundred and seventy billion dollars. That surpassed upstream oil and gas investment for the first time in history. Goldman Sachs projects data center power demand will grow a hundred and sixty-five percent by 2030. The IEA forecasts nine hundred and forty-five terawatt-hours — growing four times faster than every other sector combined.

The oil companies read these numbers too. And they are not sitting still.
scenes.visual: Clean data visualization — two rising lines on a timeline. 'Data Center CapEx' in electric blue, 'Upstream Oil Investment' in orange-red. The lines cross in 2025, DC pulling ahead. The crossover point pulses.
scenes.image_prompt: Clean modern data visualization on a dark navy background showing two trend lines on a timeline from 2018 to 2030, one line in electric blue labeled Data Center CapEx rising steeply upward, one line in orange-red labeled Upstream Oil Investment rising more slowly and then flattening, the two lines cross at a point marked 2025 which glows and pulses with a bright white circle, the blue line pulls decisively ahead after the crossover, clean sans-serif typography, financial chart aesthetic, 16:9 widescreen, no other text
scenes.text_overlay: 2025: DC CapEx ($770B) surpassed oil investment — FIRST TIME EVER / DC power: +165% by 2030 (Goldman Sachs) / 945 TWh by 2030, 4x faster growth (IEA)
scenes.music: Decisive shift, forward-moving — the story just changed gear

scenes.title: The Majors Move In
scenes.narration:
Chevron is building four gigawatts of gas-fired power foundries — their word — co-located directly with data centers. Behind the meter. No grid required. Their CEO called it a natural fit — 'it really does make good sense.' ExxonMobil is constructing a one point two gigawatt gas plant with ninety percent carbon capture, purpose-built for a hyperscaler data center. Their CEO's pitch to investors: 'we will bring this on faster than anybody else.'

TotalEnergies has signed contracts with Google to power Dutch data centers and a six hundred and ten gigawatt-hour deal with Data4 in Spain. Equinor created an entirely new business unit in April 2025 — just called it 'Power' — citing growing demand from AI and data centers. And in March of this year, Aker BP and Armada put the first AI data center on a live offshore oil platform on the Norwegian Continental Shelf. They called it a 'reference system.' That is an oil industry term. It means: a template to copy everywhere.
scenes.visual: Dark world map with oil major logos appearing at project locations — Chevron, ExxonMobil, TotalEnergies, Equinor, Aker BP. Lines of light connect each logo to its data center project site. The map fills with activity.
scenes.image_prompt: Dark world map in a modern flat design style showing the global energy-to-data-center pivot, five glowing amber-gold dots marking major project locations: US Gulf Coast for Chevron and ExxonMobil, Netherlands and Spain for TotalEnergies, Norwegian Continental Shelf for Equinor and Aker BP, thin lines of golden light connecting each dot to nearby coastal infrastructure, the dots pulse with energy suggesting active investment, dark navy ocean background, corporate strategy map aesthetic, authoritative and decisive, 16:9 widescreen, no text labels
scenes.text_overlay: Chevron — 4GW 'power foundries' / ExxonMobil — 1.2GW + 90% carbon capture / TotalEnergies — Google + Data4 / Equinor — new 'Power' unit / Aker BP + Armada — 1st AI DC on live platform (Mar 2026)
scenes.music: Confident, authoritative — these are not startups

scenes.title: The Stranded Energy
scenes.narration:
The oilfield services companies are moving even faster. Baker Hughes has booked one point two gigawatts of data center work. Halliburton's VoltaGrid subsidiary signed a two point three gigawatt deal with Oracle. SLB's digital division hit one billion dollars in annual recurring revenue — up a hundred and twenty-one percent year over year. Halliburton's CEO put it bluntly: 'The demand for power and AI is like nothing I have ever seen.'

And the energy is already there. Armada estimates six gigawatts of stranded energy exists in the United States alone — gas that is being flared or shut in because there is no pipeline to take it anywhere. That is enough to power hundreds of data centers. Shell's latest scenarios predict data centers will consume five thousand terawatt-hours by 2050 — roughly eight percent of all global electricity.

The oil companies do not need to be convinced. They have the platforms, the gas, the workforce, the marine logistics, and the decommissioning budgets. The question is not whether they will enter the data center business. It is whether they are already too late — or just early enough.
scenes.visual: Night shot of offshore platforms with gas flares burning. The flares dim and are replaced by blue LED glow from compute containers on the platform decks. Orange waste becomes blue value. The transformation is visual and literal.
scenes.image_prompt: Dramatic night scene of an offshore oil platform silhouetted against a dark sky, the traditional orange gas flare at the top is dimming and transitioning into a cool blue LED glow emanating from modern computing containers visible on the platform deck below, the color shift from warm orange waste to cool blue technology value is the central visual metaphor, the ocean reflects both colors creating a gradient from orange to blue on the water surface, cinematic and transformative, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.text_overlay: Baker Hughes — 1.2GW DC work / Halliburton/VoltaGrid — 2.3GW for Oracle / SLB Digital — $1B ARR, +121% YoY / 6GW stranded US energy (Armada) / Shell 2050: DCs consume 5,000 TWh

scenes.title: The Real Choice — Montage
scenes.narration:
The infrastructure is there. The power is there. The cooling is literally infinite. The workforce already exists. The oil companies are already writing checks. The economics work today, not in 2036. The environmental math is not just neutral — it is regenerative.

Every argument for putting data centers in outer space is an argument that works better in inner space. And there is one argument that outer space cannot make at all: that it takes care of the people and the industries that spent their lives building the offshore world we already have.
scenes.visual: Montage — wind turbines at sunset, underwater DC capsule, ROV maintenance, submarine cable being laid, worker's hands on crane console, gas platform with compute containers, oil company logo next to DC logo. Quick cuts, building energy.
scenes.image_prompt: Cinematic montage composition showing the future of ocean computing in a single wide frame: an offshore wind turbine at sunset in the background, a floating data center platform in the mid-ground with compute containers on deck, underwater data center capsules visible through the transparent ocean surface in the foreground, a small human figure in high-vis gear visible on the platform deck suggesting human scale and involvement, an oil company hard hat visible in the corner of the frame, warm golden sunset light, inspiring and forward-looking, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
scenes.music: Energy peaks — the evidence is overwhelming

scenes.title: The Close
scenes.narration: We do not need to solve the hardest problem in engineering to build the next generation of data centers. We need to solve the obvious one. And the companies that built the offshore world? They already know. Inner space is not waiting anymore. It is moving.
scenes.visual: Calm wide shot of ocean at golden hour. Single offshore wind turbine in distance. On the horizon, the silhouette of an oil platform — its gas flare replaced by soft blue glow of compute. Peaceful. Enormous. Obvious. Already happening.
scenes.image_prompt: Calm wide cinematic shot of the open ocean at golden hour, deep blue water stretching to the horizon, a single modern offshore wind turbine stands in the left middle distance, on the right horizon the dark silhouette of an oil platform is visible but its traditional gas flare has been replaced by a soft blue LED glow suggesting computing equipment, the sky is painted in warm gold and deep blue, the mood is peaceful and confident and inevitable, photorealistic, 16:9 widescreen, no text
